pattern: Two pointers separated by exactly k-1 steps let you find the k-th node from the front and, once the leader reaches the end, the k-th node from the back simultaneously, so a single pass finds both swap targets.
hints: You need the k-th node counting from the front and the k-th node counting from the back -- finding the second one without knowing the length up front is the real trick.

Walk a pointer k-1 steps ahead to mark the front node, then start a second pointer at the head and advance both together until the first pointer hits the last node -- the second pointer is now exactly k-th from the end.

Once you have references to both nodes, just swap their val fields (not the nodes themselves) and return the original head; if the two references are the same node, swapping its value with itself is a harmless no-op.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, odd length, k picks distinct front/back nodes
single node, k=1 swaps a node with itself
k picks the exact middle of an odd-length list, front and back node are the same
k equals the list length, swap tail with head
two-node list, k=1 swaps the only pair
long uniform list to keep the two-pointer walk honest

stress.description: random-length lists with random values and a k drawn from the valid range for that length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    vals = [rng.randint(0, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [vals, k]
stress.note: k always within [1, n] as required by the constraints

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def swapNodes(self, head, k):
        vals = []
        node = head
        while node:
            vals.append(node.val)
            node = node.next
        n = len(vals)
        vals[k - 1], vals[n - k] = vals[n - k], vals[k - 1]
        dummy = ListNode(0)
        tail = dummy
        for v in vals:
            tail.next = ListNode(v)
            tail = tail.next
        return dummy.next
